description: Universal device configuration system supporting MQTT, WebSocket, and Modbus TCP protocols. Adds a multi-step setup wizard, device profiles, connection testing, register mapping, and a device management dashboard to enable any compatible microcontroller to interface with rustRoast.

userStories.title: Create device and connection configuration database schema
userStories.description: As a system administrator, I want a persistent data model for devices, device profiles, and connection configurations so that device setup survives server restarts and supports multiple protocol types.
acceptanceCriteria: New migration creates `devices` table with columns: id TEXT PK, name TEXT NOT NULL, device_id TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, profile_id TEXT FK, status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending' (pending/active/disabled/error), description TEXT, location TEXT, created_at DATETIME, updated_at DATETIME, last_seen_at DATETIME

New migration creates `device_profiles` table with columns: id TEXT PK, name TEXT NOT NULL, description TEXT, default_control_mode TEXT DEFAULT 'manual', default_setpoint REAL, default_fan_pwm INTEGER, default_kp REAL, default_ki REAL, default_kd REAL, max_temp REAL DEFAULT 240, min_fan_pwm INTEGER DEFAULT 100, telemetry_interval_ms INTEGER DEFAULT 1000, created_at DATETIME, updated_at DATETIME

New migration creates `device_connections` table with columns: id TEXT PK, device_id TEXT FK NOT NULL, protocol TEXT NOT NULL (mqtt/websocket/modbus_tcp), enabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT 1, priority INTEGER DEFAULT 0, config JSON NOT NULL, created_at DATETIME, updated_at DATETIME

New migration creates `modbus_register_maps` table with columns: id TEXT PK, device_id TEXT FK NOT NULL, register_type TEXT NOT NULL (input/holding/coil/discrete), address INTEGER NOT NULL, name TEXT NOT NULL, data_type TEXT NOT NULL (uint16/int16/float32/uint32/int32/bool), byte_order TEXT DEFAULT 'ABCD', scale_factor REAL DEFAULT 1.0, offset REAL DEFAULT 0.0, unit TEXT, description TEXT, writable BOOLEAN DEFAULT 0
Foreign key constraints defined with ON DELETE CASCADE for device_connections and modbus_register_maps referencing devices
Indexes created on devices(device_id), device_connections(device_id, protocol), modbus_register_maps(device_id)
cargo build succeeds with no warnings
cargo test passes
userStories.notes: The devices table is separate from the existing in-memory device_registry cache. The cache continues to track live MQTT telemetry, while this table stores persistent configuration. The device_connections.config column stores protocol-specific JSON: for MQTT it holds topic_prefix and qos; for WebSocket it holds url and reconnect_interval; for Modbus TCP it holds host, port, unit_id, and poll_interval_ms.

userStories.title: Create device and device profile Rust models
userStories.description: As a backend developer, I want typed Rust structs for all device-related database tables so the API can serialize and deserialize device configurations safely.
acceptanceCriteria: Device struct with sqlx::FromRow derive matching the devices table schema
DeviceProfile struct with sqlx::FromRow derive matching device_profiles table
DeviceConnection struct with sqlx::FromRow derive matching device_connections table, config field stored as serde_json::Value
ModbusRegisterMap struct with sqlx::FromRow derive matching modbus_register_maps table
DeviceWithConnections response struct combining Device + Vec<DeviceConnection> for API responses
CreateDeviceRequest, UpdateDeviceRequest, CreateDeviceProfileRequest, UpdateDeviceProfileRequest request structs with appropriate Option fields
CreateConnectionRequest struct with protocol enum (Mqtt/WebSocket/ModbusTcp) and config as serde_json::Value
MqttConnectionConfig, WebSocketConnectionConfig, ModbusTcpConnectionConfig typed config structs with Serialize/Deserialize
DeviceStatus enum: Pending, Active, Disabled, Error with Display/FromStr implementations
Protocol enum: Mqtt, WebSocket, ModbusTcp with Display/FromStr and serde support
ModbusRegisterType enum: Input, Holding, Coil, Discrete
ModbusDataType enum: Uint16, Int16, Float32, Uint32, Int32, Bool
cargo build succeeds with no warnings
cargo clippy passes
userStories.notes: MqttConnectionConfig: { topic_prefix: String, qos: u8 }. WebSocketConnectionConfig: { url: String, reconnect_interval_ms: u64 }. ModbusTcpConnectionConfig: { host: String, port: u16, unit_id: u8, poll_interval_ms: u64 }.

userStories.title: Implement device service with CRUD operations
userStories.description: As a backend developer, I want a DeviceService that handles all database operations for devices, profiles, connections, and register maps so that route handlers stay thin.
acceptanceCriteria: DeviceService struct with SqlitePool, following the same pattern as RoastSessionService
list_devices(status_filter: Option<DeviceStatus>) returns Vec<Device>
get_device(id) returns Option<DeviceWithConnections> including all connections
get_device_by_device_id(device_id) returns Option<DeviceWithConnections>
create_device(req) creates device with generated UUID, returns Device
update_device(id, req) updates device fields, returns Option<Device>
delete_device(id) deletes device and cascading connections/register maps, returns bool
create_profile(req) and list_profiles() and get_profile(id) and delete_profile(id) for device profiles
add_connection(device_id, req) creates a connection entry for the device
update_connection(connection_id, req) updates connection config
remove_connection(connection_id) deletes a connection
set_register_map(device_id, registers: Vec<CreateRegisterMapEntry>) replaces the full register map for a device
get_register_map(device_id) returns Vec<ModbusRegisterMap>
update_device_status(id, status) updates the status field
update_last_seen(device_id) updates last_seen_at to now
DeviceService added to AppState
cargo build succeeds, cargo test passes, cargo clippy passes
userStories.notes: The set_register_map method should DELETE all existing maps for the device then INSERT the new ones in a transaction. This is simpler than individual add/remove operations for the register map editor.

userStories.title: Add device management REST API endpoints
userStories.description: As a frontend developer, I want REST endpoints for managing devices, profiles, connections, and register maps so the dashboard can perform full device configuration.
acceptanceCriteria: GET /api/devices returns list of all configured devices with status filter query param
GET /api/devices/:id returns device with all connections and profile info
POST /api/devices creates a new device (protected)
PUT /api/devices/:id updates device fields (protected)
DELETE /api/devices/:id deletes device (protected)
GET /api/device-profiles returns list of device profiles
GET /api/device-profiles/:id returns single profile
POST /api/device-profiles creates profile (protected)
DELETE /api/device-profiles/:id deletes profile (protected)
POST /api/devices/:id/connections adds a connection to device (protected)
PUT /api/devices/:id/connections/:conn_id updates connection (protected)
DELETE /api/devices/:id/connections/:conn_id removes connection (protected)
GET /api/devices/:id/register-map returns Modbus register map
PUT /api/devices/:id/register-map replaces full register map (protected)
All endpoints use AppError for error responses with consistent JSON shape
Read endpoints are public, mutation endpoints require API key auth
cargo build succeeds, cargo clippy passes
userStories.notes: Follow the same route organization pattern as sessions.rs and profiles.rs. Add a new routes/devices.rs file. Register routes in routes/mod.rs with the same public/protected split.

userStories.title: Add connection testing endpoints
userStories.description: As a user configuring a new device, I want to test each connection type before saving so I can verify the device is reachable and responding correctly.
acceptanceCriteria: POST /api/devices/test-connection accepts { protocol: 'mqtt', config: { topic_prefix, ... }, device_id } and checks for recent telemetry from that device_id in the telemetry cache within last 30 seconds, returns { success: bool, message: string, latency_ms: Option<u64> }

POST /api/devices/test-connection with protocol 'modbus_tcp' attempts a tokio-modbus TCP connection to the specified host:port, reads input registers 0x0000-0x0001 (bean_temp), returns success with the read values or failure with error message, includes a 5-second timeout
POST /api/devices/test-connection with protocol 'websocket' attempts a WebSocket handshake to the specified URL, waits up to 5 seconds for a message, returns success/failure
All test operations are non-destructive (read-only)
Connection test failures return 200 with success: false (not 4xx/5xx), since a failed test is a valid response
cargo build succeeds, cargo clippy passes
userStories.notes: For MQTT testing, we check the in-memory telemetry cache rather than subscribing to a topic, since the server is already subscribed to roaster/+/telemetry. For Modbus, use tokio_modbus::client::tcp::connect with a timeout. Add tokio-modbus to Cargo.toml dependencies.

userStories.title: Integrate Modbus TCP server into the backend
userStories.description: As an integrator using SCADA/HMI software, I want the rustRoast server to expose a Modbus TCP server so external systems can read telemetry and write control values using standard industrial protocols.
acceptanceCriteria: Add tokio-modbus dependency to crates/server/Cargo.toml
New module services/modbus.rs implements a Modbus TCP server using tokio_modbus::server::tcp::Server
Server binds to configurable address via RUSTROAST_MODBUS_ADDR env var, default 0.0.0.0:502
Modbus server disabled by default, enabled when RUSTROAST_MODBUS_ADDR is set

Service implementation reads input registers from the telemetry cache: registers 0x0000-0x0001 = bean_temp (float32), 0x0002-0x0003 = env_temp (float32), 0x0004-0x0005 = rate_of_rise (float32), 0x0006 = heater_pwm (uint16), 0x0007 = fan_pwm (uint16)

Service implementation reads holding registers for control values: 0x0000-0x0001 = setpoint (float32), 0x0002 = fan_pwm_setpoint (uint16), 0x0003 = heater_pwm_setpoint (uint16), 0x0004 = control_mode (uint16), 0x0005 = heater_enable (uint16)
Writing to holding registers publishes corresponding MQTT control commands (setpoint, fan_pwm, heater_pwm, mode, heater_enable)
Writing to holding register 0x000C (emergency_stop) with value 1 triggers emergency stop via MQTT
Modbus server shares AppState with Axum server for unified data access
Background task started in main.rs alongside MQTT consumer, respects cancellation token
cargo build succeeds, cargo clippy passes
userStories.notes: Context7: tokio-modbus server uses a Service trait with call() method. Each TCP connection gets its own Service instance via a factory closure. The Service reads from Arc<RwLock<HashMap>> in the existing telemetry cache. For writes, publish MQTT commands using the existing MqttService. The default device_id for Modbus register reads is configurable or uses the first device in the registry. Float32 values are encoded as IEEE 754 big-endian across 2 consecutive registers.

userStories.title: Update MQTT consumer to use device configuration
userStories.description: As a system administrator, I want the MQTT consumer to use persistent device configuration instead of only the in-memory cache so that configured devices are tracked properly and new devices trigger auto-discovery.
acceptanceCriteria: On telemetry received, MQTT consumer calls device_service.update_last_seen(device_id) to update the persistent last_seen_at
If a telemetry message arrives from an unknown device_id (not in devices table), a new device entry is auto-created with status 'pending' and a default MQTT connection config derived from the topic
Auto-created devices have name set to the device_id and description 'Auto-discovered via MQTT'
GET /api/devices/discovered endpoint returns devices with status 'pending' for the setup wizard
Devices with status 'disabled' have their telemetry cached but not recorded to session_telemetry
Last-seen updates are debounced to at most once per 10 seconds per device to avoid excessive DB writes
cargo build succeeds, cargo clippy passes, cargo test passes
userStories.notes: Use a simple HashMap<String, Instant> for last-seen debouncing. The auto-discovery feature means users see new devices appear in the wizard as soon as they power on and start publishing MQTT telemetry.

userStories.title: Create device list page in the dashboard
userStories.description: As a user, I want a device management page that lists all configured devices with their connection status, protocol types, and quick actions so I can manage my fleet of roasters.
acceptanceCriteria: New route at /devices with +page.svelte and +page.ts load function
Load function fetches GET /api/devices using SvelteKit's fetch
Page displays a table/card list of devices with columns: name, device_id, status badge, protocol icons, last seen (relative time), actions
Status badges use color coding: green for active, amber for pending, gray for disabled, red for error
Protocol icons show which connections are configured (MQTT, WS, Modbus) using lucide-svelte icons (Radio for MQTT, Wifi for WebSocket, Network for Modbus)
'Add Device' button in the page header links to the device setup wizard
Each device row has a link to the device detail/edit page
Pending devices section shown separately at top with 'Configure' action buttons
Navigation updated in +layout.svelte to include Devices link with lucide Monitor icon between Sessions and Profiles
Dark theme styling consistent with existing pages (bg-card, border-border, etc.)
npm run check passes, npm run build succeeds
userStories.notes: Follow the same page patterns as sessions/+page.svelte. Use relative time display for last_seen (e.g., '2 minutes ago', 'offline').

userStories.title: Create device setup wizard - Step 1: Discovery and identification
userStories.description: As a user setting up a new device, I want the first step of the wizard to show discovered devices or let me manually enter a device ID so I can begin configuration without knowing technical details.
acceptanceCriteria: New route at /devices/new with +page.svelte implementing a multi-step wizard
Wizard uses $state for current step index and accumulated form data
Step indicator sidebar/header shows all steps with current step highlighted: 1. Device, 2. Connections, 3. Configuration, 4. Review
Step 1 shows two sections: 'Discovered Devices' list fetched from GET /api/devices/discovered, and 'Manual Setup' form
Discovered devices show device_id, IP (from status), firmware version, signal strength, with a 'Select' button
Manual setup has fields: device_id (required), name (required), description (optional), location (optional)
Selecting a discovered device auto-fills the device_id and name fields
Device profile dropdown populated from GET /api/device-profiles, with 'None' option
'Next' button disabled until device_id and name are filled
Dark theme styling consistent with existing pages
npm run check passes, npm run build succeeds
userStories.notes: The wizard is a single SvelteKit page with conditional rendering per step ({#if step === 0}...{/if}). Form data persists across steps in a $state object. No server-side form actions needed since this is an SPA; use the API client for submission.

userStories.title: Create device setup wizard - Step 2: Connection configuration
userStories.description: As a user, I want to configure one or more communication protocols for my device with connection testing so I can verify the device is reachable before completing setup.
acceptanceCriteria: Step 2 shows protocol toggle cards for MQTT, WebSocket, and Modbus TCP with enable/disable toggles
MQTT section (enabled by default for discovered devices): topic prefix field (auto-populated as 'roaster/{device_id}'), QoS dropdown (0, 1, 2)
WebSocket section: URL field with placeholder 'ws://device-ip:port/ws', reconnect interval field (ms)
Modbus TCP section: host field, port field (default 502), unit ID field (default 1), poll interval field (default 1000ms)
Each protocol section has a 'Test Connection' button that calls POST /api/devices/test-connection
Test button shows loading spinner during test, green checkmark with 'Connected' on success, red X with error message on failure
At least one protocol must be enabled and tested successfully to proceed to Step 3
'Back' button returns to Step 1 preserving form data, 'Next' button advances to Step 3
Dark theme styling, form inputs use bg-input, border-border, text-foreground classes
npm run check passes, npm run build succeeds
userStories.notes: Connection test results are stored in $state so the user sees the status without re-testing. The test-connection endpoint is separate from device creation -- we test before saving. Use lucide-svelte icons: CheckCircle2 for success, XCircle for failure, Loader2 with animate-spin for loading.

userStories.title: Create device setup wizard - Step 3: Device configuration
userStories.description: As a user, I want to configure control defaults, safety limits, and optionally define a Modbus register map so my device operates within safe parameters from the start.
acceptanceCriteria: Step 3 has collapsible sections: 'Control Defaults', 'Safety Limits', 'PID Parameters', and conditionally 'Modbus Register Map'
Control Defaults section: default control mode radio (manual/auto), default setpoint (number input, 0-300), default fan PWM (number input, 0-255)
Safety Limits section: max temperature (number input, default 240), minimum fan PWM for heater operation (number input, default 100)
PID Parameters section (collapsed by default): Kp, Ki, Kd number inputs with sensible defaults (15.0, 1.0, 25.0)
Modbus Register Map section only shown if Modbus TCP was enabled in Step 2
Register map editor shows a table with columns: address (hex), name, type (dropdown), data type (dropdown), byte order (dropdown), scale, offset, unit, writable toggle
'Load Default Map' button populates the table with the standard coffee roaster register layout from the hardware integration guide
Add/remove row buttons for custom register entries
All number inputs have min/max validation matching the ranges in the hardware integration guide
'Back' and 'Next' buttons preserve all form data across steps
npm run check passes, npm run build succeeds
userStories.notes: The 'Load Default Map' populates registers matching the conventions from the hardware integration guide: input registers for temperatures/status, holding registers for setpoint/control. Users can customize individual rows. Collapsible sections use a $state boolean per section.

userStories.title: Create device setup wizard - Step 4: Review and save
userStories.description: As a user, I want a review step that summarizes my entire device configuration before saving so I can verify everything is correct.
acceptanceCriteria: Step 4 displays a read-only summary of all configuration from Steps 1-3 organized in sections
Device info section: name, device_id, description, location, selected profile
Connections section: list of enabled protocols with their configuration details
Configuration section: control defaults, safety limits, PID parameters
Modbus register map section (if applicable): summary table of configured registers
Connection test status shown next to each protocol (passed/not tested)
'Back' button returns to Step 3, 'Save Device' button submits the full configuration
Save action calls POST /api/devices to create device, then POST /api/devices/:id/connections for each enabled protocol, then PUT /api/devices/:id/register-map if Modbus registers were configured
On success: redirect to /devices with a success notification
On error: show error message inline without losing form data
Save button shows loading state during submission
npm run check passes, npm run build succeeds
userStories.notes: The save operation requires multiple sequential API calls. If any call fails after the device is created, show a partial success message and redirect to the device edit page so the user can fix the remaining configuration.

userStories.title: Create device detail and edit page
userStories.description: As a user, I want to view and edit an existing device's configuration on a single page with tabbed sections so I can update settings without re-running the full wizard.
acceptanceCriteria: New route at /devices/[id] with +page.svelte and +page.ts load function
Load function fetches GET /api/devices/:id
Page has tabbed navigation: Overview, Connections, Configuration, Register Map
Overview tab: device info (editable name, description, location), status badge, profile selector, last seen time, quick actions (enable/disable/delete)
Connections tab: list of configured connections with edit/remove buttons, 'Add Connection' button, test connection button per connection, inline editing of connection parameters
Configuration tab: editable control defaults, safety limits, PID parameters (same fields as wizard Step 3, but with individual 'Save' button per section)
Register Map tab (only if Modbus connection exists): same table editor as wizard with 'Save Map' button
All edits call the appropriate PUT/POST API endpoints
Delete device button with confirmation dialog
'Apply to Device' button in PID section publishes PID values via POST /api/roaster/:device_id/control/pid
Dark theme consistent with other pages
npm run check passes, npm run build succeeds
userStories.notes: Follow the ThingsBoard pattern of tabbed interface for progressive disclosure. The Overview tab is the default landing. Each tab manages its own save state independently.

userStories.title: Create device profile management UI
userStories.description: As a user managing multiple roasters, I want to create and manage device profiles that define default configurations so I can quickly onboard new devices with consistent settings.
acceptanceCriteria: New route at /devices/profiles with +page.svelte and +page.ts
Page lists all device profiles with name, description, number of devices using each profile

'Create Profile' button opens a form (inline or modal) with fields: name, description, default_control_mode, default_setpoint, default_fan_pwm, default PID values (Kp/Ki/Kd), max_temp, min_fan_pwm, telemetry_interval_ms
Each profile has edit and delete actions
Delete shows confirmation and warns if devices are using the profile
Profile creation calls POST /api/device-profiles
Profile deletion calls DELETE /api/device-profiles/:id
Dark theme styling consistent with existing pages
npm run check passes, npm run build succeeds
userStories.notes: Keep this page simple. Device profiles are templates, not complex entities. A straightforward list + create/edit form is sufficient.

userStories.title: Add device selector to session creation and dashboard
userStories.description: As a user, I want the dashboard and session controls to use the configured device list instead of raw device_id strings so the interface is friendlier and uses device names.
acceptanceCriteria: SessionControls.svelte device_id input replaced with a dropdown populated from GET /api/devices?status=active
Dropdown shows device name with device_id in parentheses, e.g., 'My Roaster (esp32-001)'
Main dashboard page (+page.svelte) shows a device selector when multiple active devices exist
Selected device_id stored in a reactive variable that drives the telemetry WebSocket filter and control API calls
If only one active device exists, it is auto-selected without showing the dropdown
If no active devices exist, show a message linking to /devices/new to set up a device
npm run check passes, npm run build succeeds
userStories.notes: This connects the device configuration system to the existing roasting workflow. The telemetry WebSocket already receives messages for all devices; the frontend just needs to filter by device_id.

userStories.title: Add Modbus register map presets for common devices
userStories.description: As a user setting up a Modbus device, I want to select from preset register maps for common coffee roaster controllers so I don't have to manually enter every register address.
acceptanceCriteria: New file lib/constants/modbus-presets.ts exports an array of register map presets
Preset: 'rustRoast Standard' matching the register layout from the hardware integration guide (input registers for sensors, holding registers for control)
Preset: 'Artisan Compatible' matching the Modbus register layout used by Artisan roasting software (common PID controller addresses)
Preset: 'Custom (Empty)' starting with a blank register map
Register map editor in wizard Step 3 and device edit page has a 'Load Preset' dropdown
Selecting a preset replaces the current register map with confirmation if entries exist
Each preset entry includes: address, name, registerType, dataType, byteOrder, scaleFactor, offset, unit, writable, description
npm run check passes, npm run build succeeds
userStories.notes: The Artisan compatible preset should follow the register addresses documented at artisan-scope.org/devices/modbus. The rustRoast Standard preset matches the register map in docs/hardware-integration-guide.md.

userStories.title: Add WebSocket connection support for device telemetry
userStories.description: As a developer building a custom device interface, I want the server to accept incoming WebSocket connections from devices that push telemetry directly over WebSocket instead of MQTT.
acceptanceCriteria: New WebSocket endpoint: WS /ws/device/{device_id}/telemetry accepts connections from devices
Incoming JSON messages with the same telemetry schema as MQTT are parsed and processed identically: cached in telemetry cache, persisted to telemetry table, recorded to active session telemetry
Device WebSocket connections update the device last_seen_at timestamp
Control commands for WebSocket-connected devices are sent as JSON messages on the same WebSocket connection instead of via MQTT
WebSocket device endpoint is public (no API key) but validates device_id exists in the devices table with status 'active'
Connection/disconnection events logged at INFO level with device_id
Existing /ws/telemetry endpoint (dashboard consumer) continues to work unchanged
cargo build succeeds, cargo clippy passes
userStories.notes: This provides a MQTT-free path for devices that prefer direct WebSocket communication (e.g., devices behind firewalls, browser-based simulators, or ESP32 devices using WebSocket libraries). The telemetry processing pipeline should be factored out of the MQTT handler into a shared function that both MQTT and WebSocket handlers call.

userStories.title: Factor telemetry processing into a shared service
userStories.description: As a backend developer, I want telemetry processing logic extracted from the MQTT handler into a shared service so that MQTT, WebSocket, and Modbus all use the same processing pipeline.
acceptanceCriteria: New TelemetryService in services/telemetry.rs with process_telemetry(device_id, payload_json, timestamp) method
process_telemetry handles: updating telemetry cache, persisting to telemetry table, recording to active session telemetry, updating device last_seen, updating metrics
MQTT handler (handle_telemetry) refactored to call TelemetryService.process_telemetry instead of inline SQL
WebSocket device handler (DEV-017) calls the same TelemetryService.process_telemetry
Modbus TCP polling loop (future) can call the same service
All existing telemetry behavior preserved (session recording, cache updates, metrics)
TelemetryService added to AppState for shared access
cargo build succeeds, cargo test passes, cargo clippy passes
userStories.notes: This is a refactor story. The current inline SQL in mqtt.rs handle_telemetry() should move to the new service. The MQTT handler becomes a thin wrapper that parses the payload and calls the service.

userStories.title: Add frontend API client functions for device management
userStories.description: As a frontend developer, I want typed API client functions for all device management endpoints so page components can call them without constructing fetch requests manually.
acceptanceCriteria: New file lib/api/devices.ts exports typed functions for all device endpoints
Functions: listDevices, getDevice, createDevice, updateDevice, deleteDevice
Functions: listDeviceProfiles, getDeviceProfile, createDeviceProfile, deleteDeviceProfile
Functions: addConnection, updateConnection, removeConnection
Functions: getRegisterMap, setRegisterMap
Functions: testConnection, getDiscoveredDevices
All functions accept SvelteKit's fetch parameter for SSR compatibility
TypeScript types for all request/response shapes in lib/types/device.ts
npm run check passes, npm run build succeeds
userStories.notes: Follow the pattern established in the load functions where fetch is passed as a parameter. These functions are used both in +page.ts load functions and in component event handlers.

userStories.title: Update hardware integration guide with Modbus and WebSocket sections
userStories.description: As a hardware developer, I want the integration guide to document all three connection methods (MQTT, WebSocket, Modbus TCP) so I can choose the protocol that best fits my microcontroller's capabilities.
acceptanceCriteria: docs/hardware-integration-guide.md updated with new section: 'WebSocket Connection' documenting WS /ws/device/{device_id}/telemetry endpoint, JSON format, and bidirectional control messages
New section: 'Modbus TCP Connection' documenting the standard register map, data types, byte ordering, and how to read/write registers
New section: 'Choosing a Protocol' with comparison table (MQTT vs WebSocket vs Modbus TCP) covering: use case, complexity, libraries needed, bidirectional support, firewall friendliness
Modbus register map table matches the presets defined in DEV-016
Example code snippets for WebSocket connection (Arduino/ESP32 using WebSocket library)
Example code snippets for Modbus TCP slave (using Arduino Modbus library)
Minimal implementation checklist updated to include all three protocol options
userStories.notes: Keep the existing MQTT section intact. Add the new protocol sections after it. The protocol comparison table should be honest about tradeoffs: MQTT is best for unreliable networks, WebSocket is simplest for direct connections, Modbus TCP is best for SCADA integration.
